rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 5] This tentative is issued by Hon. Ross C. Moody. Before the court is specially appearing Respondent Timothy Schell's Motion to Quash Service of Summons, specifically the Summons and Petition purportedly served on him on or about December 15, 2021. Petitioners' request to continue this hearing until they receive and file the Certificate of Service on Schell from the appropriate Canadian authority is DENIED. Respondent Schell's Request for Judicial Notice is GRANTED. Notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) This Motion to Quash was originally set for hearing on March 17, 2022. Petitioners filed a timely Opposition and supporting declarations on March 4, 2022. On March 16, 2022, before the court had posted a tentative ruling, the moving party requested the hearing be continued. The hearing was continued to July 28, 2022. On July 18, 2022, Petitioners filed Supplemental Opposition of Motion and supporting declarations. Respondent Schell filed a timely Reply on July 21, 2022, and Objections to Supplemental Evidence Submitted By Petitioners in Opposition to Motion to Quash, objecting to the supplemental declaration of Gary Coffey and the declaration of Matt Ysinga on various evidentiary grounds, and to the Supplemental Opposition as an improper filing. Per California Rules of Court, Rule 3.1300(a), "Unless otherwise ordered or specifically provided by law, all moving and supporting papers must be served and filed in accordance with Code of Civil Procedure section 1005 and, when applicable, the statutes and rules providing for electronic filing and service." Per CCP section 1005(b), "All papers opposing a motion so noticed shall be filed with the court and a copy served on each party at least nine court days, and all reply papers at least five court days before the hearing." Although "[n]o paper may be rejected for filing on the ground that it was untimely submitted for filing" the court may, in its discretion, refuse to consider a late filed paper (CRC 3.1300(d)). The Supplemental Opposition and supporting declarations filed July 18, 2022 are late filed, without a prior court order finding good cause for the late "supplemental" opposition. The court exercises its discretion in refusing to consider them. The court has and will consider the timely March 4, 2022 Opposition and supporting declarations. Respondent Schell's Objections to Supplemental Evidence Submitted are SUSTAINED IN PART insofar as the Supplemental Opposition and supporting declarations are late filed and the court will not consider them. Respondent's objections to the supplemental declaration of Gary Coffey and the declaration of Matt Ysinga are OVERULED insofar as the court's decision to not consider the late filed supplemental filings moots the objections to the supporting declarations. The Motion to Quash Service of Summons is GRANTED IN PART and DENIED IN PART. As explained below, the motion is granted with respect to argument regarding the manner of service of the December 2021 Summons at issue. However, it is denied with respect to the argument regarding this court's lack of specific personal jurisdiction over Schell.

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Quash Service Of Summons (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 5] Under CCP section 418.10(a), a defendant, on or before the last day to plead, may file a motion to "quash service of summons on the ground of lack of jurisdiction of the court over him or her []" A challenge to personal jurisdiction may be based on improper service of process due to either a defect in the method of serving the summons, or a defect in the summons that was served. The Opposition acknowledges that service was not proper under the Hague Convention on service of process, and states that service has now been properly effectuated. This subsequent service does not salvage the initial insufficient service attempt, however. The Motion to Quash Service of Summons is GRANTED IN PART based on a defect in the method of service. Specially appearing Respondent Schell also seeks to quash the service of summons based on this court's alleged lack of personal jurisdiction over him. The Motion is DENIED IN PART as to the court's alleged lack of personal jurisdiction. A trial court can grant a motion to quash on grounds of defect in method of service, while still finding sufficient minimum contacts exist as a basis for specific personal jurisdiction. As discussed in Ziller Elecs. Lab GmbH v. Sup. Ct. (1988) 206 Cal. App. 3d 1222, 1226, 1229, "A trial court has jurisdiction to make an initial determination as to its own subject matter or in personam jurisdiction or lack thereof when challenged by a "specially appearing" defendant. [] Since jurisdiction to consider an issue is coextensive with jurisdiction to determine it, it follows that trial courts have jurisdiction to make a determination as to each jurisdictional criterion that is challenged by the motion to quash. This jurisdiction is not threatened by a negative determination of one criterion nor by the particular priority in which the criteria are determined. [citation.]" California courts may exercise personal jurisdiction on any basis consistent with the Constitutions of California and the United States. (CCP section 410.10.) Under Probate Code section 17004, "The [Probate] court may exercise jurisdiction in proceedings under this division on any basis permitted by Section 410.10 of the Code of Civil Procedure." More specifically, the exercise of jurisdiction over a nonresident defendant comports with the US and California constitutions "if the defendant has such minimum contacts with the state that the assertion of jurisdiction does not violate 'traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice.'" (Vons Companies, Inc. v. Seabest Foods, Inc. (1996) 14 Cal.4th 434, 444, quoting Internat. Shoe Co. v. Washington (1945) 326 U.S. 310, 316.) The parties do not dispute the issue of whether this court had general personal jurisdiction over Timothy Schell, a resident of Canada, who does not live or work in California. The dispute in this motion practice instead centers on whether this court may exercise specific jurisdiction over Mr. Schell in the instant probate matter. Per the California Supreme Court in Vons, a court may exercise specific jurisdiction over a nonresident defendant only if a three prong test is met: (1) "the defendant has purposefully availed himself or herself of forum benefits" [citation]; (2) "the 'controversy is related to or "arises out of "[the] defendant's contacts with the forum'" [citation]; and (3) "'the assertion of personal jurisdiction would comport with" fair play and substantial justice "'" [citation.] (Vons, supra, 14 Cal.4th at 446-448)

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Quash Service Of Summons (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 5 of 5] Based on the verified pleadings taken as evidence, and supporting materials submitted under penalty of perjury, Petitioners have met their initial burden to establish there is a substantial connection between the Petition's claims and Schell's alleged forum activities, in that the claim for return of the vehicles "arises from" Respondent's alleged activities in California. Once the Court has concluded that plaintiff has satisfied the purposeful availment and relatedness requirements, the Court must ask the third prong of the specific personal jurisdiction analysis, "whether the assertion of specific jurisdiction is fair." (Vons, 14 Cal.4th at 475-476.) "Once facts showing minimum contacts with the forum state are established, however, it becomes the defendant's burden to demonstrate that the exercise of jurisdiction would be unreasonable." (Id. at 449.) In making this determination, the Court must consider the burden on the defendant, the interests of the forum State, and the plaintiff's interest in obtaining relief. It must also weigh "the interstate judicial system's interest in obtaining the most efficient resolution of controversies; and the shared interest of the several States in furthering fundamental substantive social policies." (Id. at p. 476, citing Asahi Metal Industry Co. v. Sup. Ct (1987) 480 U.S. 102, 113.) To defeat jurisdiction, the defendant must present a compelling case that exercising jurisdiction would be unreasonable. (Jayone Foods, Inc. v. Aekyung Industrial Co. Ltd. (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 543, 553; Buskirk, supra, at 536; Vons, supra, at 476.) Respondent Schell has not met this burden. Ordering Schell to appear in this Court to file a response or opposition to the Petition will not require physically leaving his work and young children in Canada. He need not travel to California personally to represent himself. If Schell wishes to appear at a hearing, remote appearances are now available under California statutes and local rules of court - and are still strongly encouraged in this department. Although the Motion references the "unique burdens" a foreign national may face if haled into a local court, it does not discuss what unique burdens would be placed on Respondent Schell apart from the burden of physical travel to San Francisco, which the court has already addressed. It is not unfair or unreasonable to hale Schell into this Court to file a response or opposition to the Petition given (1) his alleged availment of California as the forum to obtain the vehicles, and (2) the Petition's Probate Code section 850 claim for return of the vehicles arising from his alleged purchase of them in San Francisco, from McLaughlin. The probate estate from which these vehicles were allegedly unlawfully removed is being administered in San Francisco, and the alleged harm to the estate occurred in California. For these reasons, both the State and the Petitioners have a compelling interest in settling these claims in California, rather than some other forum. Petitioners are ordered to lodge with the clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with all substantive provisions of this tentative ruling, as published above, by the time set for this hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the court day before the hearing. Remote appearance is available consistent with San Francisco Superior Court Local Rules, rule 14.1(D).

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Quash Service Of Summons (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 5] Respondent Schell states in his supporting declaration at paragraphs 5, 6 and 7 that he did not: communicate with Respondent McLaughlin, negotiate the purchase price of the vehicles, arrange their transportation, or enter into "any contract involving California law, citizens, or property." But the verified Petition alleges that Schell personally wired payment for the vehicles to Respondent McLaughlin in California in November and December 2020 (Pet. at par. 5). Exhibit 4 to the March 2022 declaration of Gary Coffey in support of the Opposition, signed under penalty of perjury, consists of two Bills of Sale dated November 16, 2020, listing Kate McLaughlin as seller and Tim Schell as buyer, of (1) a 1969 Chevrolet Camaro, VIN number 124379N647693 for the sum of $100,000, and (2) a 1969 Chevrolet Camaro, VIN number 124379N642835 for the sum of $500,000. Both Bills of Sale ostensibly bear the signatures of McLaughlin and Schell. Exhibit 5 to the March 2022 declaration of Gary Coffey in support of the Opposition, signed under penalty of perjury, consists of two wire transfer receipts, dated November 13, 2020 and December 7, 2020 respectively, and both ostensibly bearing the signature of Respondent Schell. The receipt dated November 13, 2020 lists "Timothy Schell" as the "Ordering Customer" and lists an address in Queensville, Ontario. "Kate Mclaughlin "is listed as the "Beneficiary Customer" and lists a residential San Francisco address and a San Francisco Citibank branch address. The Payment Amount is listed as $10,000. The receipt dated December 2, 2020 also lists "Timothy Schell" as the "Ordering Customer" and lists an address in Queensville, Ontario. "Kate Mclaughlin "is listed as the "Beneficiary Customer" and lists a residential San Francisco address and a San Francisco Citibank branch address. The Payment Amount is listed as $590,000. Per Coffey's declaration, Schell himself emailed copies of these Bills of Sale and wire transfer receipts to Coffey after their initial May 5, 2021 telephone conversation (paras 8, 9, 10). Based on the evidence presented in the verified petition and Coffey's declaration, the court finds that Petitioners have met their burden on the first prong to establish by a preponderance of the evident Respondent Schell's "purposeful availment" of the benefits of the forum. Mr. Schell is not being haled into this jurisdiction because of "fortuitous or attenuated contacts or because of the unilateral activity of another party" (Buskirk, supra, at 532). Rather, he is being haled into this jurisdiction because he allegedly personally entered into sales contracts-two bills of sale- and directed significant monetary payments--$600,000 wired in 2 installments-- to a person in California, to purchase goods--two vintage Camaros-- located in California. For purposes of this motion, the Court concludes that Schell "purposely availed" himself of the benefits of California as a forum in purchasing the subject vehicles from McLaughlin in California, and wiring payment to McLaughlin's Citibank account listed with a San Francisco bank branch address, whereby he should have reasonably expected to be subject to its jurisdiction. The second analytical prong looks for "a substantial connection between [Respondent's alleged] forum activities and the [Petitioners'] claims." (Buskirk, supra, 53 Cal. App. 5th at 536, citing Vons, supra, 14 Cal.4th at 452.) Petitioners' 850 claim for return of the vehicles "arises from" Schell's alleged contacts with the forum: entering into an agreement with Respondent McLaughlin to buy and sell the vehicles (Pet. at par. 5.)

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Quash Service Of Summons (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 5] The Petitioners have the initial burden. "When a defendant moves to quash service for lack of personal jurisdiction, the plaintiff must establish by a preponderance of the evidence the facts justifying the exercise of jurisdiction. The burden then shifts to the defendant to demonstrate exercising jurisdiction would be unreasonable." (Buskirk v. Buskirk (2020) 53 Cal. App. 5th 523, 530, quoting Jayone Foods, Inc. v. Aekyung Industrial Co. Ltd. (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 543, 553.) "Jurisdictional facts must be proved by admissible evidence. This generally requires declarations by competent witnesses. A properly verified complaint may be treated as a declaration for this purpose." (Buskirk v. Buskirk (2020) 53 Cal. App. 5th 523, 535, quoting Weil & Brown, Cal. Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2020) 3:387.) Here, the Petition is verified by both Petitioners under penalty of perjury in accordance with CRC 7.103 ("All pleadings filed in proceedings under the Probate Code must be verified"). As discussed in Buskirk v. Buskirk (2020) 53 Cal. App. 5th 523, 532, "[t]he first prong of the three-part test is "purposeful availment": have defendants purposefully availed themselves of forum benefits? We consider whether the defendants' conduct connects them to the forum in a meaningful way. [citation] Defendants purposefully avail themselves of a forum's benefits if they intentionally direct their activities at a forum such that, by virtue of the benefit the defendants receive, they should reasonably expect to be subject to jurisdiction there. [citation] By focusing on the defendants' reasonable expectations, this requirement ensures defendants will not be haled into a jurisdiction solely because of fortuitous or attenuated contacts or because of the unilateral activity of another party. [citation.]" As stated in Vons Companies, Inc. v. Seabest Foods, Inc. (1996) 14 Cal. 4th 434, 446, "[T]he requisite forum contact [involves] a nonresident defendant who " 'deliberately' has engaged in significant activities with a State [citation] or has created 'continuing obligations' between himself and residents of the forum [citation]", concluding that in such cases the defendant "manifestly has availed himself of the privilege of conducting business [in the forum], and because his activities are shielded by 'the benefits and protections' of the forum's laws it is presumptively not unreasonable to require him to submit to the burdens of litigation in that forum as well." Respondent is correct that the verified petition states at paragraph 6 that "Bulaw, acting as McLaughlin's agent, travelled to California to take possession of the Cameros on behalf of Schell." An alleged or implied agency relationship between Bulaw and Mclaughlin and/or Bulaw and Schell is, at best, unclear in the pleadings (see Pet. at par. 5, 6). That sentence of the verified pleading may be treated as admissible evidence. That sentence alone, however, does not eliminate all the other admissible evidence found in the verified pleading and properly verified supporting materials. Respondent Schell states in his Declaration in Support of Motion to Quash that he has never been to San Francisco, even on vacation (at par. 4). A party need not have physically entered a forum state to be subject to specific personal jurisdiction there, however. (See, e.g., Burger King Corp. v. Rudzewicz (1985) 471 U.S. 462, 473-474.)
